era_context: The early 14th century sees the Yuan Dynasty in China under Emperor Renzong (r. 1311–1320), a period of relative stability and cultural achievement. In Europe, the Avignon Papacy under Pope Clement V (r. 1305–1314) is in its final years; Pope John XXII will be elected in 1316. The beginning of the Great Famine (1315–1317) marks a major demographic and economic crisis in northern Europe. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia.

events.title: Beginning of the Great Famine in Northern Europe
events.region: Northern Europe (England, Scotland, Scandinavia, Low Countries, France)
events.description: The Great Famine began in 1315 and lasted until 1317, with the most severe conditions in 1316–1317. The famine was caused by a series of poor harvests due to unusually wet and cold weather. Grain prices rose dramatically, and widespread malnutrition and disease followed. The famine had profound effects on population, economy, and social stability across northern Europe. Mortality rates in some regions reached 5–10% of the population. The famine is extensively documented in contemporary chronicles and is confirmed by modern climate and demographic research.

sources.name: Contemporary chronicles (Langtoft, Murimuth, Froissart, Scottish chronicles, Low Countries sources)
Modern historiographic consensus based on climate data, tree rings, and demographic analysis

events.certainty_note: The Great Famine is extensively documented in contemporary sources and confirmed by modern climate and demographic research. The dating to 1315–1317 is firmly established.

events.title: Death of Pope Clement V
events.description: Pope Clement V died on 20 April 1314 (not 1315, but the vacancy lasted until August 1316 when John XXII was elected). The death of Clement V marked the end of an era of papal history. Clement V had moved the papal seat to Avignon in 1309, beginning the Avignon Papacy. His death created a vacancy that lasted over two years, during which the College of Cardinals was divided and unable to elect a successor quickly.
events.certainty_note: The death of Clement V is precisely dated to 20 April 1314, not 1315. However, the papal vacancy extended into 1315 and 1316.

events.title: Continued Consolidation of Ottoman Power in Anatolia
events.description: Under Osman I (r. 1299–1323/4), the Ottoman beylik continued its territorial expansion and consolidation of power in northwestern Anatolia. By 1315, Ottoman forces had captured several important fortified towns and were establishing themselves as a major regional power. The expansion was gradual but steady, taking advantage of the decline of Byzantine power and the fragmentation of other Turkish states.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (later compilations such as the Menakib-i Osman)

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in this period is well-attested, though specific events for 1315 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Reign of Emperor Renzong in Yuan Dynasty
events.description: Emperor Renzong (Ayurbarwada, r. 1311–1320) was ruling the Yuan Dynasty in 1315. His reign was characterized by relative stability and cultural achievement. Renzong promoted Confucian learning and restored the civil service examination system, marking a shift toward more traditional Chinese governance within the Mongol-ruled empire. His reign is considered one of the more successful periods of Yuan rule.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Yuan Dynasty records are well-preserved. Emperor Renzong's reign and his policies are firmly established.

events.title: Continued Conflict in the Wars of Scottish Independence
events.description: Following the Scottish victory at Bannockburn (1314), Robert I of Scotland sought to consolidate Scottish independence. In 1315, Scottish forces conducted military operations and raids, seeking to pressure England into recognizing Scottish independence. The Wars of Scottish Independence continued as a major conflict in the British Isles.
key_figures: Robert I of Scotland (King, 'Robert the Bruce')
events.certainty_note: The broader conflict is well-attested, though specific events for 1315 are not always precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Great Famine is sometimes portrayed as a uniform catastrophe across all of northern Europe, but recent scholarship suggests regional variation. Some areas were more severely affected than others, and recovery began at different times in different regions. The mortality rates, while significant, may have been somewhat lower than older estimates suggested. The famine's causes are attributed to climate change (the onset of the Little Ice Age), but the exact mechanisms and regional variations are still debated among historians and climate scientists.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1315 is dominated by Western European sources, particularly chronicles from England, Scotland, and France. The Great Famine is extensively documented because of its dramatic impact on populations and economies. Chinese sources are well-preserved and provide detailed information about the Yuan Dynasty. Islamic and African sources are underrepresented. The reliability of dating for specific military events is lower than for major famines or political upheavals. The famine's documentation is particularly rich because of its widespread impact and the detailed accounts in contemporary chronicles.

graph_edges.note: Beginning of the Great Famine in 1315 led to continued crisis in 1316
Great Famine continued and peaked in 1317
Death of Clement V created papal vacancy that ended with election of John XXII in 1316
